title: What Does My Lottery Fantasy Say I Want?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for reading the windfall fantasy as a message about unmet needs, and finding the real thing underneath the rescue story
excerpt: A journal prompt set for the pull toward windfall thinking. The prompts treat the Lottery fantasy not as a flaw to correct but as a map: the specific version you imagine tells you what you actually want, and the real thing is nearly always more achievable than the fantasy.
sourceNotes: Original journal prompts written for homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you notice the windfall or rescue fantasy surfacing regularly, and want to understand what real, achievable need is sitting underneath it.

content.text: A journal prompt set for the windfall fantasy. The prompts do not treat the fantasy as a failure of mindset. They treat it as a signal — and ask what specific, real thing it is pointing toward.
New to journal prompts as practice? Read
Describe your Lottery fantasy in detail. What do you do first? What changes?
Write the whole thing. Do not edit or qualify it.
What is the feeling you are chasing in the fantasy? Not the money — the feeling.
Safety? Freedom? Relief? Permission? Write the exact feeling.
What is the most important thing you would do with the windfall money, if you strip away the drama?
Name the single most important one. Write how achievable it actually is without the lottery.
What would have to be true for the feeling in the fantasy to be available to you without the windfall?
Write the conditions. Write whether any of them are already within reach.
If the windfall never came, what would the most direct path to that feeling actually be?
Write the direct answer. Start one step.
